Client 2: And I’m Susa—same, but I also want it in plain English. Like, what does it mean month to month.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow, then we’ll hit assets, any debts, and protection like insurance. Aver, what do you consider your household gross income per year right now?
Client 1: All-in, household gross is 175300 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And Susa, when you think about your typical spending, what’s the total monthly expenses you try to keep it around?
Client 2: We aim for 4700 a month. Sometimes it creeps a bit, but 4700 is the target.

Client 1: All-in, household gross is 175300 a year.

Advisor: Okay. And on the debt side—mortgage, car loans, credit cards—what are your monthly debt payments total?
Client 2: Zero. Monthly debt payments are 0.

Client 2: And I’m Susa—same, but I also want it in plain English. Like, what does it mean month to month.

Client 2: Zero. Monthly debt payments are 0.
Advisor: Nice. So no required payments each month. Now shifting to what you’ve built—investments and accounts you can draw on—about how much do you have in investable assets total?
Client 1: Investable assets total are 895000.
Advisor: Great—one sec, I’m just noting that down. When you say investable assets, that’s excluding the home value—right?
Client 1: Right, excluding the house.

Client 2: Yeah, it’s kind of a bank account but through insurance—value is 45850.
Advisor: Got it. And then the home—just so we cover everything listed—your primary residence is held on the advisor platform, and what are you using as the current value?
Client 1: We’ve got the primary residence at 207000.
Advisor: Perfect. On liabilities, you already said no monthly debt payments—just confirming, do you have any loans outstanding at all right now?
Client 1: No, loans outstanding are 0.

Advisor: Great, thanks. And what state do you live in—just the state for planning assumptions, no address details.
Client 2: It’s listed as Other for our state of residence.
Advisor: Okay, got it. Now, when markets get choppy, how would you describe your overall risk tolerance—conservative, moderate, aggressive, somewhere in there?
Client 1: I’d say moderate.
Advisor: Moderate—perfect. Susa, does that feel right to you too, or would you say you’re a little more cautious than that?

Advisor: Aver, starting with the home: do you have any mortgage balance outstanding right now, even a small one?
Client 1: No—mortgage balance outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Okay. Any home equity line of credit or other loan tied to the house?
Client 1: Nope, nothing like that. It’s all at 0.
Advisor: Great. Susa, outside the mortgage—credit cards, car loans, student loans, personal loans—what’s your non-mortgage debt outstanding total?

Advisor: Perfect—next time let’s dig into your goals and how you want to use that extra cash flow.
Advisor: Totally. Next time we’ll go deeper on goals, but before we leave the basics—Aver, Susa—can I confirm your marital status?
Client 1: We’re married.
Advisor: Great, thanks. And what state do you live in—just the state for planning assumptions, no address details.
Client 2: It’s listed as Other for our state of residence.

Advisor: Moderate—perfect. Susa, does that feel right to you too, or would you say you’re a little more cautious than that?
Client 2: I’m a little more cautious emotionally, but yeah, overall moderate works if we’ve got a cushion.
Advisor: That makes sense. Last admin-ish thing: how are we working together—are you engaging me through an RIA, a registered investment advisor, or through a brokerage relationship?
Client 1: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything from what we covered: we talked through income, assets, no liabilities, and that disability policy—anything else you want to make sure we hit next time?

Advisor: Hi Aver, hi Susa—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make this meeting feel useful for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Aver. Useful is: confirm we’re on track for retirement and that our monthly spending isn’t drifting.
Client 2: And I’m Susa—same, but I also want it in plain English. Like, what does it mean month to month.

Advisor: Totally fine—ballpark helps, but can we pin the exact figure you’ve been using so the math lines up?
Client 2: Yes—use 39050 as my gross annual income.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. One more admin detail, and then I’ll stop peppering you with questions.
Advisor: In our notes we label each person as Client 1 and Client 2 just so we don’t mix accounts—Susa, you’re Client 2, correct?

Client 1: I’m the primary.
Advisor: Got it—primary. And for your work, what would you call your occupation group in plain terms—like finance, tech, operations, that kind of bucket?
Client 1: Operations.
Advisor: Perfect. When you say operations, is that more like running day-to-day processes, project delivery, people management—just so I’m picturing it right?

Client 2: Yeah, it’s kind of a bank account but through insurance—value is 45850.

Client 2: Monthly. It’s monthly.
Advisor: Okay. And the amount—do you track that as a monthly number or an annual total?
Client 1: Annual total. The annual amount is 61900.
Advisor: Thanks—hang on, I’m just writing that down. And just to be clear, that 61900 is for the interest & dividends stream, joint, paid monthly—right?
Client 1: Right.

Advisor: Hi Aver, hi Susa—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make this meeting feel useful for you?
